title: The Prince
summary: Niccolò Machiavelli's short treatise on political power, written in 1513, which set aside the question of how rulers ought to behave and asked instead how power is actually acquired, maintained, and lost.
hook: Every political thinker before Machiavelli asked how rulers ought to govern. Machiavelli asked how they actually do. That shift — from moral prescription to political observation — is why The Prince was put on the Catholic Church's Index of Forbidden Books and why it is still read five centuries later.

beginner.theText:
In 1513, a Florentine official named Niccolò Machiavelli was out of a job. The Medici family had returned to power in Florence and expelled him from the government he had served for fourteen years. He retired to a small farm, wrote letters to friends, and — in a few months — produced a short book about political power that would eventually make him famous.

The book is called The Prince. It is addressed to Lorenzo de' Medici, the new ruler of Florence, as a kind of advice manual. But it is unlike every advice manual that came before it.

Before Machiavelli, books advising rulers focused on virtue. A good prince should be honest, merciful, generous, pious. The assumption was that good character produced good rule.

Machiavelli threw this out. He observed that rulers who always behaved virtuously tended to lose power to those who did not. A prince who always kept his promises would be exploited by those who broke theirs. A prince who was always merciful would be seen as weak. A prince who was always generous would exhaust his treasury.

What mattered, Machiavelli argued, was results. A ruler who could hold his state, defend his people, and maintain order was a successful ruler — regardless of how he achieved it. Cruelty used once and decisively was better than cruelty spread out over time. It was better to be feared than loved, if you had to choose.

People have been arguing about whether Machiavelli was right ever since. Some think he was simply describing reality. Others think he was advocating something monstrous. The word 'Machiavellian' — meaning calculating, ruthless, and willing to deceive — entered the language as an insult. Machiavelli himself would probably not have been surprised.
beginner.momentOfMaking:
The Prince was written in a moment of personal and political crisis. Florence had lost its republic and returned to Medici rule. Machiavelli — a committed republican who had served Florence loyally — found himself on the wrong side of history.

Italy in 1513 was a battlefield. French armies, Spanish armies, and Papal forces competed for territory. Small Italian states were conquered and reconquered. Machiavelli had watched it happen from the inside, as a diplomat who negotiated with Cesare Borgia, met with the Holy Roman Emperor, and observed how power actually worked at close range.

The Prince reflects all of this. It is not an abstract philosophical exercise. It is the work of a man who spent fourteen years watching politics operate and then had the time, the anger, and the clarity to write down what he had seen.
beginner.whatItClaims:
The Prince's central claim is that political success depends on the effective use of power, not on virtue as traditionally understood.

Machiavelli distinguishes between how people live and how they ought to live. He argues that a ruler who only does what he ought will be ruined by those who do what they must. The world as it is does not reward innocence.

He also argues that a ruler must know how to use both law and force — the human way and the animal way. He should imitate the lion (who cannot protect himself from traps) and the fox (who cannot defend himself against wolves). A ruler who is only a lion or only a fox will fail.

And he argues that fortune — chance, circumstance, the unpredictable flow of events — determines about half of human affairs. The other half is up to human action, which he calls virtù: not virtue in the moral sense but energy, adaptability, and the capacity to seize opportunities.
beginner.interpretiveEcosystem:
The Prince has been read in radically different ways over five centuries.

Some readers think Machiavelli was a cold-eyed realist describing how power actually works — a political scientist centuries ahead of his time. Others think he was providing a how-to manual for tyrants. Still others, most famously the philosopher Jean-Jacques Rousseau, argued that The Prince was actually a satire — secretly written to warn ordinary people about how princes behave, rather than to advise princes on how to behave.

In the 20th century, scholars began taking seriously Machiavelli's other works — particularly the Discourses on Livy, where he argues at length for republican self-government. This made The Prince look different: not a celebration of princely power but a practical manual for a specific emergency, while Machiavelli's deeper political commitments lay elsewhere.
beginner.usedAndWeaponized:
The Prince has been invoked to justify almost every form of political behavior. It has been cited by kings, dictators, revolutionaries, and corporate executives. Hitler reportedly kept a copy by his bed. Stalin was said to have read it carefully.

But it has also been cited by those who wanted to understand and resist power. Gramsci, imprisoned by Mussolini, wrote about The Prince at length in his prison notebooks, arguing that Machiavelli's real heir was not the individual tyrant but the modern political party — a collective force that could unite a fragmented people.

The word 'Machiavellian' has become so thoroughly pejorative that it now means simply 'cynically manipulative.' This is a distortion. Machiavelli was doing something more complicated — and more uncomfortable — than simply telling rulers how to be cruel.

general.theText:
The Prince is a short book — seventy or eighty pages in most editions — but its intellectual impact is disproportionate to its length. Written in a matter of months in 1513, circulated in manuscript for nearly two decades, and published in 1532 (five years after Machiavelli's death), it fundamentally changed how Europeans thought about politics.

What makes The Prince distinctive is its method. Machiavelli explicitly sets aside the question of how rulers ought to govern — which had been the central question of the entire tradition of political advice literature — and asks instead how power is actually acquired, held, and lost. His evidence is historical: Roman emperors, contemporary Italian princes, Cesare Borgia. His conclusions are derived from observation, not from philosophy or theology.

This methodological move had enormous consequences. Machiavelli effectively separated political analysis from moral philosophy and created something like modern political science — the study of power as it actually operates rather than as it ought to operate. Whether this was a liberation or a catastrophe is still debated.
general.momentOfMaking:
Machiavelli wrote The Prince in the specific context of the Italian Wars — the decades-long competition between France, Spain, and the Holy Roman Empire for dominance over the Italian peninsula. Florence had oscillated between republican government and Medici rule, and in 1512 the Medici returned with Spanish military backing, dismantled the republic, and dismissed the officials who had served it.

Machiavelli was one of those officials. He had spent fourteen years as second chancellor of the Florentine republic, managing diplomatic missions and military affairs. He had met Cesare Borgia in person — the ruthless, brilliant son of Pope Alexander VI who briefly seemed on the verge of unifying central Italy under his control. He had watched Borgia succeed through a combination of violence, deception, and extraordinary political skill, and then collapse when fortune turned against him.

The Prince was, among other things, an attempt to make sense of what Machiavelli had seen — and to offer his services to the new rulers of Florence by demonstrating that he understood how power actually worked. The dedication to Lorenzo de' Medici was not just flattery; it was a job application.
general.whatItClaims:
The Prince makes several claims that were, and remain, controversial.

First, that political morality must be distinguished from private morality. A ruler must sometimes do things that a good private person should never do — lie, break promises, use violence — because the alternative is to be destroyed by those who will. This is not a celebration of these acts but a cold assessment of their necessity.

Second, that appearances matter more than reality in politics. A ruler need not have the qualities associated with virtue — mercy, faith, integrity, humanity, religion — but he must appear to have them. What is important is that people think well of him, not that he is in fact good.

Third, that fortune and virtù are the two primary forces in political life. Fortune — the unpredictable flow of events — determines perhaps half of human affairs. The other half is up to virtù: not virtue in the classical moral sense but something closer to vigor, intelligence, adaptability, and decisiveness. The ruler who can adjust his approach to circumstances will succeed; the ruler who is always the same will fail when circumstances change.
general.interpretiveEcosystem:
The interpretive history of The Prince is nearly as interesting as the text itself. Three broad readings have dominated.

The realist reading, dominant from the 16th century onward, takes The Prince at face value as a description of how power actually works and a practical guide for rulers. On this reading, Machiavelli was simply telling the truth about politics — a truth that idealists prefer not to acknowledge.

The republican reading, developed most systematically in the 20th century by scholars including J.G.A. Pocock and Quentin Skinner, emphasizes the continuity between The Prince and Machiavelli's other works — especially the Discourses on Livy, a sustained defense of republican government. On this reading, The Prince was a practical emergency manual, not a statement of Machiavelli's political philosophy, which was fundamentally republican.

The anti-tyranny reading, suggested by Rousseau and developed by various scholars since, treats The Prince as a kind of encoded warning — a book that tells people in power what they actually do, thereby exposing them. This reading is difficult to verify but has had persistent appeal.
general.usedAndWeaponized:
The Prince has been weaponized across the political spectrum for five centuries, which is itself evidence of how richly ambiguous it is.

Monarchs used it to justify the concentration of power. Revolutionaries used it to understand how power could be seized. Corporate strategists in the 20th century translated its advice into business language. Academic international relations theorists used it as a founding text for political realism.

The most influential 20th-century reading was Antonio Gramsci's. Writing in a Fascist prison in the 1930s, Gramsci argued that the modern equivalent of Machiavelli's prince was not an individual ruler but a political party — specifically, the Communist Party, which could become the collective force capable of unifying and directing the Italian working class. This reading rescued Machiavelli from purely conservative appropriations and placed him in the tradition of democratic politics.

The word 'Machiavellian' has come to mean cynical manipulation without ethical constraint. This is a reduction. What Machiavelli actually argued was that political necessity sometimes requires doing things that private morality prohibits — and that a ruler who cannot make this distinction will fail. Whether this is wisdom or moral catastrophe is the question the book has been forcing readers to confront for five hundred years.

advanced.theText:
The scholarly rehabilitation of Machiavelli in the 20th century hinged on reading The Prince against the Discourses on Livy — and recognizing that they are in genuine tension with each other, not secretly harmonious. The Discourses is a long defense of republican government, arguing that a republic is stronger and more durable than a principality because it can draw on the full energy of its citizens. The Prince argues that a founder or reformer of a state must act alone and ruthlessly — that the multitude is incompetent for political foundation, which requires a single decisive will.

The tension is real. Machiavelli resolved it, partially, by treating The Prince as addressing an emergency — the specific Italian crisis of his moment — rather than as a statement of general principle. The founder must act alone; once the republic is established, civic participation becomes both possible and necessary. But this resolution requires the reader to supply what the text does not quite say, and scholars continue to debate whether Machiavelli had a coherent political philosophy or whether his works reflect irreducible tensions between his commitments.

What is not in doubt is that Machiavelli created a new way of thinking about politics. By separating political analysis from moral prescription — by asking what is rather than what ought to be — he made possible the tradition that would eventually produce modern political science, international relations theory, and the study of power as an empirical phenomenon.
advanced.momentOfMaking:
The intellectual context of The Prince was the tradition of 'mirror of princes' literature — advice books for rulers stretching back to antiquity and running through the medieval period. These books uniformly recommended virtue: justice, prudence, fortitude, temperance. They assumed that good character produced good governance and that the ruler's moral development was the central concern of political education.

Machiavelli was thoroughly educated in this tradition, which is why his departure from it was so striking. He did not reject virtue as a value — he rejected the assumption that rulers could afford to be consistently virtuous in a world where other rulers were not. The Prince is legible as a critique of the 'mirrors of princes' tradition from within: here is what those books leave out; here is what actually happens when their advice is followed.

The Italian context matters in a second way. Machiavelli lived through the period when Italy was becoming the proving ground for modern state-building — the first region of Europe where territorial states competed through sophisticated diplomacy, professional armies, and deliberate statecraft rather than feudal allegiances. He had a ringside seat to the emergence of the modern state, and The Prince reflects that observation.
advanced.whatItClaims:
The philosophical core of The Prince is the claim that political ethics must be autonomous from private ethics — that what a ruler must do to maintain a state is not reducible to what a private person must do to be virtuous. This position is technically known as 'political realism' or the doctrine of 'dirty hands.'

The dirty hands problem — named by Michael Walzer in a famous 1973 essay — is the claim that political action sometimes requires doing something morally wrong for the sake of a larger political good, and that the agent who does it is genuinely guilty of wrongdoing even if the action was necessary. This is different from the simple claim that the ends justify the means. It acknowledges that the means are wrong while insisting that sometimes they must be used anyway.

Machiavelli's version of this problem is starker than most subsequent formulations because he does not apologize for it. He presents the necessity of political transgression not as a tragic burden but as a political skill — something that effective rulers must develop. This lack of hand-wringing is part of what has always disturbed readers, and it is what makes The Prince an irreducibly uncomfortable book rather than simply a historical curiosity.
advanced.interpretiveEcosystem:
The debate between the 'evil Machiavelli' and the 'republican Machiavelli' interpretations has structured scholarship since Isaiah Berlin's famous essay 'The Originality of Machiavelli' (1972). Berlin argued that neither reading was fully adequate — that Machiavelli's real innovation was recognizing that there are genuinely incompatible values, that the political and the moral good are sometimes irreconcilably in conflict, and that anyone who denies this is either naive or dishonest.

Berlin's reading has been influential but contested. Quentin Skinner, in Machiavelli (1981) and later work, argued that The Prince is best understood within the context of humanist rhetoric and the 'mirrors of princes' tradition — that Machiavelli's departures from that tradition were more calculated and contextual than Berlin's reading suggests. Harvey Mansfield's Machiavelli's Virtue (1996) pushed in a different direction, arguing that Machiavelli's real target was the entire Western tradition of virtue ethics, Christian and classical alike, and that he was attempting a much more fundamental philosophical revolution than republican scholars acknowledge.

The interpretive ecosystem around The Prince is itself a measure of its richness: a text that has sustained five centuries of serious disagreement about what it means has clearly touched something unresolved in Western political thought.
advanced.usedAndWeaponized:
The most illuminating use of The Prince in the 20th century is not its invocation by dictators — which tells us less about the text than about its reputation — but Gramsci's reading in the Prison Notebooks. Gramsci argued that Machiavelli's prince was a 'concrete fantasy' — an image of leadership that crystallized the need for a collective political will in fragmented Italy. The modern equivalent, for Gramsci, was the Communist Party: a 'modern prince' that could play the role of organizing collective will that no individual could play in modern mass politics.

This reading did something important: it separated Machiavelli's analysis of the requirements of political leadership from his prescription that a single individual must provide it. Gramsci accepted the analysis while replacing the individual prince with a collective institution. Whether this is faithful to Machiavelli or a creative misreading is less important than the intellectual move it enabled — it showed that Machiavellian political thinking could be democratized.

The continuing invocation of The Prince in corporate management literature — the genre that produces books titled 'Machiavelli on Management' — represents the opposite move: stripping political analysis of its political content and reducing it to individual manipulation tactics. This reading is a reduction that Machiavelli himself would have recognized as precisely the kind of thinking he was criticizing: treating politics as a technical problem of individual skill rather than a question about how communities govern themselves.

foundation.theStory: The Prince poses a problem that has not gone away: can politics be studied as it is rather than as it should be, and if so, what follows from the answer? Machiavelli's answer was that studying politics as it is reveals that the ethics of the ruler cannot simply be the ethics of the private person — that political necessity sometimes requires what private morality forbids. Whether this is a profound insight, a moral catastrophe, or an empirical observation about the conditions of political life is a question that five centuries of readers have not settled.
foundation.theDebate: Historians and political theorists disagree about whether Machiavelli had a coherent political philosophy. The 'civic humanist' school, associated with J.G.A. Pocock and Quentin Skinner, argues that Machiavelli was fundamentally a republican thinker and that The Prince must be read in light of the Discourses. Critics of this view, including Harvey Mansfield, argue that the tension between the two works is real and that the Discourses does not resolve it — that Machiavelli's thought is irreducibly contradictory or requires a more radical reading than the civic humanists provide.
foundation.whyItMatters: The Prince is the founding text of political realism — the tradition in political theory and international relations that treats power, interest, and the limits of morality as the primary objects of political analysis. Understanding The Prince is essential for understanding every subsequent realist tradition, from Hobbes and Hume through Weber and Morgenthau to contemporary international relations theory. It is also essential for understanding the 'dirty hands' problem in political philosophy — the question of whether political actors can be morally required to do things that are morally wrong.
questions: Is Machiavelli's separation of political ethics from private ethics a description of how politics actually works, a prescription for how rulers should behave, or both — and does the difference matter?
Does reading The Prince alongside the Discourses on Livy resolve the tension between them, or does it reveal an irreducible contradiction in Machiavelli's political thought?

interpretation.scholarlyConversation: The major fault lines in Machiavelli scholarship are: (1) the relationship between The Prince and the Discourses — do they represent a coherent philosophy or an irresolvable tension? (2) the extent to which The Prince should be read as a product of its humanist context versus as a radical break from it; (3) the 'evil Machiavelli' versus 'republican Machiavelli' debate, structurally organized by Berlin's 1972 essay; (4) the question of whether Machiavelli was doing political science, political philosophy, or political rhetoric. The Cambridge School (Pocock, Skinner) and the Straussian school (Mansfield) represent the sharpest contemporary disagreement.
interpretation.evidenceAndLimits: The textual evidence for interpreting The Prince is rich but bounded — Machiavelli's letters, his other works, and the humanist context he was operating in provide substantial interpretive resources. What the evidence cannot resolve: Machiavelli's intentions, which he was careful not to state directly. The republican reading is plausible but requires supplying what the text withholds. The radical reading (Machiavelli as fundamental critic of Western moral tradition) is also plausible but requires reading against the grain of many passages. The text sustains multiple interpretations, which is itself a datum about what kind of text it is.
interpretation.takingAPosition: A strong scholarly argument about The Prince must engage directly with the Discourses and take a position on how the two works relate. It must address the dirty hands problem — whether Machiavelli is making a descriptive or normative claim about the relationship between political and private ethics. It must engage with the Cambridge School / Straussian debate rather than simply choosing a side. And it must account for the text's reception history — why The Prince has meant such different things to such different readers — as evidence about what is genuinely ambiguous in it.
questions: Does the 'republican Machiavelli' interpretation require ignoring or explaining away what The Prince actually says, or does it represent a genuine resolution of the tension between the two works?

Is the dirty hands problem — the idea that political actors may be morally required to do things that are morally wrong — a coherent position, or does it collapse either into 'the ends justify the means' or into a simple description of tragedy?

comparativeNarrative.name: The Church's View
comparativeNarrative.content: The Prince was placed on the Index of Forbidden Books in 1559, where it remained. The Church's objection was not primarily to Machiavelli's political realism but to his treatment of religion as a political instrument — something a ruler should appear to have rather than actually possess. For the Church, this reduced religion to a technique of social control, stripping it of its truth claims and its authority over rulers. The condemnation also reflected the Church's broader anxiety about the new secular political thinking that was reshaping European governance — thinking that treated political authority as grounded in power and consent rather than divine sanction.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Republican View
comparativeNarrative.content: Within the Italian republican tradition — of which Machiavelli was himself a part — The Prince was read as an emergency manual, not a political philosophy. Republics produce better soldiers, more energetic governments, and more durable institutions than principalities: this is what Machiavelli argued at length in the Discourses. The Prince addressed a specific crisis — Italy's fragmentation and vulnerability to foreign conquest — by asking what kind of individual leadership could consolidate and defend a state. This reading, developed by 20th-century scholars, sees no contradiction between The Prince and Machiavelli's broader republican commitments because it treats them as operating at different levels of political analysis.

comparativeNarrative.name: The Postcolonial View
comparativeNarrative.content: The Prince's reception outside Europe reveals the text's ambiguity in a different register. For political thinkers in colonized or recently decolonized societies, Machiavelli's analysis of how power is seized, maintained, and defended from stronger rivals had direct practical relevance. Frantz Fanon's analysis of colonial violence drew on a broadly Machiavellian framework. Various independence movements have engaged with Machiavelli's analysis of how new states are built under conditions of conflict — not as an endorsement of ruthlessness but as a sober acknowledgment of the conditions under which political founding happens. The Prince read from the periphery looks different than The Prince read from the center of established power.

rabbitHole.label: Power
rabbitHole.reason: The Prince is among the founding texts of the Western tradition of thinking about political power as an object of analysis rather than moral prescription — the entry on Power places Machiavelli in that longer tradition.

rabbitHole.label: The Renaissance
rabbitHole.reason: The Prince emerged from Renaissance Florence and its distinctive culture of political experiment, humanist learning, and artistic patronage — understanding the Renaissance context is essential for understanding what Machiavelli was responding to.

rabbitHole.label: Thomas Hobbes
rabbitHole.reason: Hobbes, writing a century after Machiavelli, pushed the separation of political analysis from moral philosophy further — arguing that in the state of nature, there is no morality at all, only power. The line from Machiavelli to Hobbes is one of the clearest in Western political thought.

rabbitHole.label: Napoleon
rabbitHole.reason: Napoleon is the closest historical equivalent to Machiavelli's prince — a ruler who rose through virtù and force, reshaped a political landscape, and fell when fortune turned against him.

rabbitHole.label: The Art of War — Sun Tzu
rabbitHole.reason: The Art of War and The Prince are the two canonical ancient texts on power and strategy — both ask how power is maintained rather than how it ought to be used, and both have been applied far beyond their original military and political contexts.

rabbitHole.label: Machiavelli — Quentin Skinner (1981)
rabbitHole.reason: The essential introduction to Machiavelli's thought — Skinner situates The Prince within its humanist context and articulates the republican reading with clarity and scholarly rigor.

reference.author: Skinner, Quentin
reference.title: Machiavelli
reference.annotation: The best starting point for serious engagement with Machiavelli. Skinner situates The Prince within the tradition of humanist advice literature it was departing from and makes the strongest case for reading it alongside the Discourses as part of a coherent republican project.

reference.author: Berlin, Isaiah
reference.title: The Originality of Machiavelli
reference.annotation: Berlin's essay organized the modern debate about Machiavelli by refusing both the 'evil Machiavelli' and the 'republican Machiavelli' readings and locating his originality in value pluralism. Essential reading even for those who disagree.

reference.author: Mansfield, Harvey
reference.title: Machiavelli's Virtue
reference.annotation: The principal alternative to Skinner's civic humanist reading. Mansfield argues that Machiavelli's target was not just the 'mirrors of princes' tradition but Aristotelian and Christian virtue ethics as a whole. Dense but rewarding.

reference.author: Gramsci, Antonio
reference.title: Prison Notebooks (selections on Machiavelli)
reference.annotation: Gramsci's engagement with Machiavelli in the Prison Notebooks produced the most creative political appropriation of The Prince in the 20th century. The argument that the modern equivalent of Machiavelli's prince is a collective political institution rather than an individual is both a reading of Machiavelli and an original contribution to political theory.

commerce.title: The Prince
commerce.author: Niccolò Machiavelli (trans. Harvey Mansfield)
commerce.note: Mansfield's translation is the most philosophically precise; his introduction articulates the Straussian reading clearly.

commerce.title: Machiavelli
commerce.author: Quentin Skinner
commerce.note: The best short introduction — 100 pages that situate The Prince in its intellectual context and make the republican reading accessible.

commerce.title: The Prince and The Discourses
commerce.author: Niccolò Machiavelli (Modern Library edition)
commerce.note: Reading both works together is essential — the tension and continuity between them is where Machiavelli's thought is richest.

popularCulture.title: House of Cards
popularCulture.description: The American adaptation follows a ruthlessly Machiavellian politician whose explicit willingness to treat power as an end in itself and morality as an instrument makes the show a sustained dramatization of Machiavellian political ethics — and of what happens when a ruler loses sight of any purpose beyond self-preservation.

popularCulture.title: The Godfather
popularCulture.description: Coppola's trilogy can be read as a sustained meditation on Machiavellian themes: the acquisition and maintenance of power, the relationship between force and consent, the costs of political founding, and what happens when virtù fails to adapt to changing fortune.

popularCulture.title: Game of Thrones
popularCulture.description: The show's sustained engagement with political power — who holds it, how it is maintained, and what it costs — draws directly on Machiavellian themes, particularly the tension between the politically effective and the morally admirable, and the dangers of rulers who cannot distinguish between them.

research.topic: The relationship between The Prince and the Discourses on Livy
research.content: Scholarship since the mid-20th century has established that The Prince and the Discourses on Livy were written in overlapping periods (both between 1513 and 1517) and that they represent genuinely different emphases rather than a simple contradiction. The Discourses is a sustained argument for republican government as stronger and more durable than princely rule. The Prince addresses the specific problem of political founding and emergency. Whether these different emphases constitute a coherent political philosophy or an unresolved tension is a matter of interpretive judgment, but the basic biographical and textual facts — that Machiavelli was simultaneously a committed republican and the author of The Prince — are not disputed.

The intellectual sources for The Prince are well documented. Machiavelli drew primarily on Roman history, particularly Livy and the Lives of the emperors, and on his own diplomatic experience — especially his four encounters with Cesare Borgia in 1502-03. The 'mirrors of princes' tradition he was departing from is also well documented: specific texts that Machiavelli knew, including Pontano's De principe and Patrizi's De regno, have been identified and their relationship to The Prince analyzed. The extent to which Machiavelli's departures from this tradition were deliberate provocations versus organic outcomes of his empirical method remains debated.

research.topic: Machiavelli's intentions in writing The Prince
research.content: What evidence would resolve this: explicit private statements by Machiavelli about his purposes in writing The Prince. Why unavailable: the letters that survive are careful and do not disclose whether The Prince was sincere advice, an ironic critique, a job application, or some combination. What can be responsibly inferred: that the dedication to Lorenzo de' Medici was partly a bid for employment; that Machiavelli's republican commitments were genuine given his consistent expression of them elsewhere; that The Prince was not simply a cynical exercise given its intellectual depth and consistency. What must remain unknowable: whether the republican reading or the realist reading better captures Machiavelli's own understanding of what he was doing.
